mapping.a_element: iterative elimination of configurations with the highest exit rate in configuration space
mapping.b_element: analysis of spectral gap and mixing time under boundary conditions in tree structures

mapping.a_element: renormalized exit barriers defined as negative logarithm of exit rates (-ln W_out)
mapping.b_element: logarithmic measures of mixing time (log-Sobolev constant, spectral gap)

mapping.a_element: broadening distribution of renormalized exit barriers leading to asymptotic exactness at large time scales
mapping.b_element: bounded spectral gap and log-Sobolev constant implying O(log n) mixing time

mapping.a_element: master equation with transition rates between configurations
mapping.b_element: Glauber dynamics for Ising model or other spin systems

brief_justification: While both papers analyze dynamical processes on discrete structures (configuration space vs. tree graphs) and use logarithmic measures of rates or mixing times, Paper A employs a strong-disorder renormalization group (RG) procedure to iteratively eliminate configurations, whereas Paper B focuses on spectral gaps and log-Sobolev constants under boundary conditions. The core mechanisms—RG-based coarse-graining vs. functional analytic bounds on mixing—are fundamentally distinct.
